topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp cardboard felt alien in my hand, a stark contrast to the usual junk mail cluttering my mailbox. It was an unsigned birthday card, the edges slightly softened, bearing a cheerful, looping script that belonged to someone else entirely. My fingers traced the ink, a strange heat rising in my chest. I tucked it quickly into my jeans pocket, the fabric suddenly feeling too tight. Every glance toward the house felt like a spotlight, exposing some deep-seated inadequacy. I spent the rest of the afternoon meticulously rearranging my bookshelf, a frantic attempt to restore order to a world that suddenly felt deeply messy and exposed.

[story 2]

I stared at the card under the harsh fluorescent light of the kitchen table. The handwriting was elegant, undeniably kind, and it was addressed to ‘Alex.’ My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. A knot tightened in my stomach, a heavy, cold weight settling low in my abdomen. I picked up a pen, intending to scribble something meaningless on the back, but the paper seemed to mock my hesitation. I pushed the card away, the movement jerky, and walked out onto the porch, staring blankly at the neighbor’s perfectly manicured lawn, wishing the pavement would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The mail slot always delivered a predictable barrage of bills and advertisements. This card, however, was an anomaly—a silent declaration from an unknown source. When I finally pulled it out, the paper seemed flimsy, almost fragile. My palms grew damp. I folded it once, twice, then unfolded it again, my gaze darting around the room as if expecting someone to materialize and judge the contents. A flush crept up my neck, warming my cheeks to an uncomfortable red. I shoved the card deep into the bottom drawer of my desk, pulling the drawer shut with unnecessary force, the click echoing loudly in the quiet apartment.

The mail slot always delivered a predictable barrage of bills and advertisements. This card, however, was an anomaly—a silent declaration from an unknown source. When I finally pulled it out, the paper seemed flimsy, almost fragile. My palms grew damp.
